conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with you in Manchester.

Since Alex is 17 (over 16), I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in approved education. Actually, he's just started an apprenticeship - he's training to be an electrician.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's an important detail.

Just to clarify: **Did Alex start his apprenticeship in England?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he started his apprenticeship here in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official guidance I've reviewed, I need to inform you of the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

The reason is that according to UK government rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England. Even though Alex is only 17 and would normally qualify for Child Benefit until age 20 if he were in approved education or training, apprenticeships in England are specifically excluded from this provision.

If you were previously receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you should contact the Child Benefit Office to inform them that he has started an apprenticeship so they can update your claim accordingly.

Is there anything else I can help you with regarding Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child 'starts an apprenticeship in England.' While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 who remain in approved education or training, apprenticeships in England specifically disqualify a child from Child Benefit eligibility. Since Alex has started an apprenticeship in England, you are not eligible to receive Child Benefit for him.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, aged 17, who lives with them in Manchester, England. Alex has just started an apprenticeship in England to become an electrician. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, regardless of the child's age (even if they are under 20). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child 'starts an apprenticeship in England.' While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 who remain in approved education or training, apprenticeships in England specifically disqualify a child from Child Benefit eligibility. Since Alex has started an apprenticeship in England, you are not eligible to receive Child Benefit for him.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, aged 17, who lives with them in Manchester, England. Alex has just started an apprenticeship in England to become an electrician. Under UK Child Benefit rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, regardless of the child's age (even if they are under 20). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
